content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 26, 2019

Glenn Spitzer
Deputy County Counsel
810 Court Street
Jackson, CA 95642

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-19-136

Dear Mr. Spitzer:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your inquiry as a request for formal assistance.² Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May Amador County Planning Commissioner Andy Byrne ("Commissioner Byrne") participate in decisions regarding AT&T applications for permits when Commissioner Byrne currently owns 106 shares of AT&T stock?

CONCLUSION

Each permit decision will need to be evaluated individually based upon the nature of the permit. However, as a small shareholder, Commissioner Byrne may take part in a permit decision involving AT&T unless the decision may result in (1) a change of AT&T's annual gross revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000 or five percent of AT&T's annual gross revenues or (2) AT&T incurring or avoiding expenses in an amount equal

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-136
Page No. 2

to $250,000 or one percent of the AT&T's annual gross revenues. Additionally, Commissioner
Byrne may not take part in the decision if he knows or has reason to know that AT&T has property
affected by the decision and there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will have a
substantial effect on the property.³

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are Deputy County Counsel with Amador County and are seeking advice on behalf of
Commissioner Byrne. Commissioner Byrne currently owns 106 shares of AT&T stock presently
valued at approximately $3,312.50. The stocks were acquired by Commissioner Byrne
approximately 10 years ago and were valued at $2,666.90 when acquired. AT&T regularly applies
for permits for telecom facilities before the Amador County Planning Commission. Commissioner
Byrne is seeking advice on whether he may participate in these decisions in his official capacity.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a
conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103.

Under Sections 82034 and 87103(a), an official has a financial interest in any business entity
in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. An
"investment" means any financial interest in or security issued by a business entity, including, but
not limited to, common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt instruments, and any
partnership or other ownership interest owned directly, or indirectly, or beneficially by the public
official. (Section 82034.)

Based on the facts provided, Commissioner Byrne has a financial interest in a business
entity worth $2,000 or more by virtue of his ownership interest in 106 shares of AT&T stock,
valued at approximately $3,312.50. Therefore, we must determine whether there will be a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on Commissioner Byrne's financial interest as a
result of the decisions before the Planning Commission involving AT&T's applications for permits
for telecom facilities.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest "is the subject of a decision if the decision involves the

³ Citations provided are to Regulation 18702.1 as adopted by the Commission on July 18, 2019. The analysis
provided in this letter applies Regulation 18702.1 as adopted.

File No. I-19-136
Page No. 3

issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlements to, or
contract with, the financial interest…" (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly
involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(6).)

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) The
materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in Regulations 18702.1 through
18702.5. Regulation 18702.1 provides a list of circumstances under which a reasonably foreseeable
effect on a business interest is material. As most pertinent to the facts provided, the foreseeable
effect of a governmental decision on a business entity in which an official has a financial interest is
material whenever the business entity initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or other request for action concerning the entity with the official's agency or the business
entity applies for a permit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or other entitlement from
the official's agency. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1)(A) and (E).)

Additionally, the financial effect upon a business entity in which an official has a financial
interest is material under Regulation 18702.1(a) whenever any of the following are met:

"(2) Gross Revenues and Assets or Liabilities. The decision may result in an
increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than:

"(A) $1,000,000; or

"(B) Five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the increase or
decrease is at least $10,000.

"(3) Expenses. The decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than:

"(A) $250,000; or

"(B) One percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in
expenses is at least $2,500.

"(4) Real Property. The official knows or has reason to know that the entity
has an interest in real property and:

"(A) The property is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision under
Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(1) through (6); or

"(B) There is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a
substantial effect on the property."

You seek advice regarding Commissioner Byrne's participation in decisions before the
Amador County Planning Commission. As discussed above, the decision before the Planning
Commission involves the approval or denial of applications for permits for AT&T, a business entity
in which Commissioner Byrne has a financial interest by virtue of his ownership interest in AT&T's

File No. I-19-136
Page No. 4

stock valued at $2,000 or more. AT&T is the subject of the decision before the Planning
Commission. Therefore, the financial effect on AT&T is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable
under Regulation 18701(a).

The financial effect of the decisions on AT&T is also material pursuant to Regulation
18702.1(a)(1)(A) and (E) cited above because the decisions involve AT&T's applications for
permits. However, based on the facts provided, the small shareholder exception in Regulation
18702.1(b) applies.

Small Shareholder Exception

The small shareholder exception applies when an official has an investment interest in a
business entity and the investment interest is valued at $25,000 or less and is less than one percent
of the entity's shares. Under the exception, an official meeting the small shareholder standard may
take part in the decision if the decision's effect on the official's investment interest in the business
entity is only material under subdivision (a)(1) or (a)(4)(A) of Regulation 18702.1. However, the
official may not take part in the decision if the decision's effect on the business entity meets the
materiality standards under subdivision (a)(2)-(3) or (a)(4)(B) of Regulation 18702.1.

In regard to a permit decision involving AT&T, Commissioner Byrne's investment interest
consists of 106 shares of stock and is valued at approximately $3,312.50, which is less than $25,000
and less than one percent of AT&T's shares. Therefore, so long as the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of the decision is not material under subdivision (a)(2)-(3) or (a)(4)(B) of
Regulation 18702.1, Commissioner Byrne may participate in the decisions under the small
shareholder exception.

However, we emphasize that each permit decision will have to be evaluated individually
prior to Commissioner Byrne taking part in the decision. Commissioner Byrne may not take part in
the decisions if the permit may result in a change meeting the materiality thresholds in Regulation
18702.1(a)(2)-(3) or (a)(4)(B).

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Katelyn Greene
Counsel, Legal Division

KG:aja

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a

meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial

on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. Interests frpm which a 
conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. 
Under Sections 82034 and 87103(a), an official has a financial interest in any business entity 
in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. An

not limited to, common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants, options, debt instruments, and any

official. (Section 82034.)

entity worth $2,000 or more by virtue of his ownership interest in 106 shares of AT&T stock, 
valued at approximately $3,312.50. Therefore, we must determine whether there will be a

result of the decisions before the Planning Commission involving AT&T' s applications for permits 
for telecom facilities.

agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest "is the subject of a decision if the decision involves the

provided in this letter applies Regulation 18702.1 as adopted.

issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlements to, or 
contract with, the financial interest ... " (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly 
involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be 
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(6).) 
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) The 
materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in Regulations 18702.1 through

effect on a business interest is material. As most pertinent to the facts provided, the foreseeable 
effect of a governmental decision on a business entity in which an official has a financial interest is 
material whenever the business entity initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,

Additionally, the financial effect upon a business entity in which an official has a financial 
interest is material under Regulation 18702.1 ( a) whenever any of the following are met: 
"(2) Gross Revenues and Assets or Liabilities. The decision may result in an 
increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or the value of the 
entity's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than: 
"(A) $1,000,000; or 
"(B) Five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the increase or 
decrease is at least $10,000. 
"(3) Expenses. The decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional 
expenses or t~ reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than: 
"(A) $250,000; or 
"(B) One percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in 
expenses is at least $2,500. 
"(4) Real Property. The official knows or has reason to know that the entity 
has an interest in real property and: 
"(A) The property is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision under 
Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(l) through (6); or 
"(B) There is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a 
substantial effect on the property." 
You seek advice regarding Commissioner Byrne's participation in decisions before the 
Amador County Planning Commission. As discussed above, the decision before the Planning 
Commission involves the approval or denial of applications for permits for AT&T, a business entity 
in which Commissioner Byrne has a financial interest by virtue of his ownership interest in AT&T's 


FileNo. I-19-136  
stock valued at $2,000 or more. AT&T is the subject of the decision before the Planning 
Commission. Therefore, the financial effect on AT&T is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable 
under Regulation 18701(a). 
The financial effect of the decisions on AT&T is also material pursuant to Regulation 
18702.1 (a)(l )(A) and (E) cited above because the decisions involve AT &T's applications for 
permits. However, based on the facts provided, the small shareholder exception in Regulation

Small Shareholder Exception 
The small shareholder exception applies when an official has an investment interest in a 
business entity and the investment interest is valued at $25,000 or less and is less than one percent 
of the entity's shares. Under the exception, an official meeting the small shareholder standard may

However, we emphasize that each permit decision will have to be evaluated individually 
prior to Commissioner Byrne taking part in the decision. Commissioner Byrne may not take part in
